nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments including reef flats, lagoons, and upper reef slopes with moderate light and water movement

decorPreferences: several inches of spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Benefits from supplemental target feeding of micro-plankton, mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, zooplankton, and small pellet foods once or twice per week in the evenings when feeding tentacles are extended. Amino acid supplements have been reported to support polyp health and coloration.

goodWith: other LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (~7-10 cm)
aggressive SPS corals in direct contact

summary: Favia (trade name) is a hardy, colorful LPS brain coral generally considered beginner-friendly. Moderate lighting, gentle-to-moderate flow, and weekly supplemental feeding support growth; sweeper tentacles require careful spacing from neighbors.
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE: The name 'Favia' is preserved here as a trade name used throughout the aquarium hobby. Following a major molecular-based reclassification published in 2014 (Huang et al., Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society), the vast majority of Indo-Pacific species formerly placed in Favia were redistributed to other genera, most prominently Dipsastraea, as well as Goniastrea, Coelastrea, and Favites. The genus Favia sensu stricto now contains only two Caribbean Atlantic species (F. fragum and F. gravida), which are not generally available in the US aquarium trade due to collection restrictions. The corals sold commercially as 'Favia' — including popular named morphs like Yellow Submarine, Wolverine, Mayan Sun, and Diversity Favia — are currently recognized as Dipsastraea sp. under the family Merulinidae (previously Faviidae). The scientificName field retains 'Favia sp.' to reflect the persisting trade convention and ensure aquarist discoverability.

CARE OVERVIEW: Favia (Dipsastraea) corals are generally considered one of the more forgiving LPS corals in the reef hobby, making them suitable for beginners and intermediate aquarists. Their large, colorful fleshy polyps make them visually striking and they tend to adapt well to a range of tank conditions within the recommended parameter windows.

LIGHTING: Generally advised to keep under moderate light conditions, roughly 75–200 PAR. Most sources suggest starting new specimens in lower-light areas of the tank (50–100 PAR) and gradually acclimating upward over 2–3 weeks. Excessively high light (>200 PAR) has been reported to cause bleaching and color loss. LED, T-5, or metal halide lighting all have been reported to work well at appropriate intensities.

FLOW: Moderate (medium) water movement is generally recommended. Low-to-moderate flow prevents detritus from settling on the coral tissue and encourages healthy polyp extension. Avoid strong direct laminar flow, which can stress the tissue.

PLACEMENT: Best placed on stable rockwork in the middle to lower portions of the aquarium. Favia corals are encrusting to dome-shaped and settle well on horizontal or angled surfaces. They have been known to extend sweeper tentacles 7–10 cm (approximately 3–4 inches) at night to sting neighboring corals; several inches of clear space around the colony is strongly recommended. Plan for moderate steady growth (roughly 2.5–7.5 cm per year under good conditions).

AGGRESSION: Semi-aggressive. Sweeper tentacle extension at night is well-documented across sources; tentacles can reach approximately 7–10 cm beyond the colony margin. Careful spacing from all neighbors is important for peaceful long-term coexistence in a mixed reef.

WATER CHEMISTRY: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium dosing is generally advised for sustained skeletal growth. Elevated nitrate (>30–40 ppm) has been reported to affect coloration and polyp extension. Moderate nutrients (nitrate 5–25 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.1 ppm) are tolerated and some sources suggest slightly elevated nutrients improve coloration.

FEEDING: Primarily photosynthetic. Supplemental target feeding once or twice per week has been reported to noticeably improve growth rate and color saturation. Feed in the evening when feeding tentacles are visible; small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, zooplankton, powdered plankton, and prepared coral foods are all accepted.

PESTS: Flatworms are occasionally observed on Favia/Dipsastraea corals. Standard coral dips (e.g., CoralRx, Revive) are generally effective; new specimens should be dipped before introduction to prevent hitchhiker introduction.

PARAMETER NOTES (cross-source summary): Temperature: averaged from LiveAquaria (72–78°F / 22–26°C) and Bulk Reef Supply (75–78°F / 24–26°C) — min 22°C adopted from LiveAquaria, max 26°C as shared upper bound. Salinity: LiveAquaria 1.023–1.025, BRS 1.025–1.026, WWC 1.024–1.026 — averaged to 1.024–1.026. Alkalinity: LA 8–12 dKH (wide), WWC 8–10, BRS 8–9.5 — averaged to 8–10 dKH. Calcium: all sources agree 400–450 ppm. Magnesium: BRS 1300–1450, WWC 1200–1350, Top Shelf 1250–1350 — averaged to 1250–1400 ppm.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) is the standard propagation method for Favia/Dipsastraea in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw; frags readily encrust on frag plugs or rubble and resume normal growth. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Favia Coral
primary.notes: Primary page returned 404; care parameters extracted from LiveAquaria search result content for the Maricultured Favia Coral listing (product 3291) and a CCGC Favia listing (6669), plus the crawled care summary text: temp 72–78°F, dKH 8–12, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, moderate lighting, moderate flow, semi-aggressive sweeper tentacles, feeding twice weekly in the evening.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Yellow Submarine Favia
additional.notes: Cross-checked all water parameters: temp 75–78°F, SG 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alk 8–9.5 dKH, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1300–1450 ppm, PAR 150–250, low-to-moderate flow, mid-to-lower placement, moderate care level. Parameters averaged with other sources.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Favia vs. Favites Blog & Product Pages
additional.notes: Cross-checked parameters: PAR 100–150, pH 8.0–8.4, alk 8–10 dKH, Ca 420–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1350 ppm, SG 1.024–1.026, feeding 3–4 times per week. Product pages confirm easy care, medium light, medium flow. Used for temperament and feeding guidance.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Favia Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic reclassification details (Favia to Dipsastraea 2014 split, Merulinidae family). Care data: PAR 50–100, low-to-moderate flow, bottom placement preferred, Ca ~425 ppm, Mg ~1350 ppm. PAR data on the conservative side; averaged with other sources to yield 75–200 range. Confirmed feeding 3x/week with mysis and powdered plankton.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Your Favia's Not a Favia
additional.notes: Used for reclassification documentation only: confirmed 2014 molecular reclassification moved most Indo-Pacific Favia to Dipsastraea; confirmed family Merulinidae (formerly Faviidae); confirmed true Favia sensu stricto now only F. fragum and F. gravida (Caribbean). Not used for husbandry parameters.
